profile.name: Thomas
profile.role_in_household: Primary earner; tends to take point on investments and long-term planning while coordinating with spouse on big decisions.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate-to-high: comfortable discussing account types (taxable vs. retirement) and investment concepts, and tends to track performance and income streams, but may gloss over insurance/protection details.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Leans into the household’s aggressive posture: willing to tolerate volatility if the plan is coherent; prefers staying invested and gets impatient with too much cash drag, but wants a clear rationale for any conservative moves.
profile.communication_style: Direct and fairly fast-paced; uses specific numbers when he remembers them and will correct small inaccuracies. Still uses hedges like "I think" and "roughly" when recalling dates or exact line items; asks pointed follow-up questions and may jump ahead to outcomes.
emotional_tendencies: gets uneasy when expenses feel out of control
profile.decision_making_style: Data-first: wants options compared side-by-side (tradeoffs, taxes, timelines), then decides quickly once he feels the logic is sound.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously optimistic: open to guidance but expects competence and transparency; trust increases when the advisor references specifics and follows through.
internal_biases: overconfidence in ability to ride out market swings
recency bias (recent market moves influence urgency)
hidden_concerns: whether the current spending level is sustainable with four kids
fear of making a big mistake with the mortgage vs. investing decision

profile.name: Jennife
profile.role_in_household: Spouse/partner; manages day-to-day family logistics and cash flow awareness, and acts as a practical counterweight on risk and monthly commitments.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate: understands budgeting and the basics of retirement accounts, but tends to think in round numbers and occasionally mixes terms (balance vs. payment, gross vs. net). Prefers plain-English explanations over jargon.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Comfortable with growth in principle but more sensitive to downside when it threatens monthly stability; prefers a visible cash buffer and may push back if the plan feels too aggressive given childcare and mortgage obligations.
profile.communication_style: Warm, collaborative, and story-based; speaks in ranges ("around," "like," "maybe") and may not recall exact figures on the spot. Will interrupt to clarify practical impacts (monthly payment, emergency fund, kids’ needs) and asks for summaries.
emotional_tendencies: stress spikes around large recurring payments
relief when there’s a clear buffer and checklist
profile.decision_making_style: Consensus-driven: wants to talk it through, understand the monthly impact, and then align with Thomas; prefers incremental changes rather than sweeping shifts.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Generally trusting but needs to feel heard; trust rises when the advisor translates recommendations into concrete next steps and acknowledges family constraints.
internal_biases: loss aversion (feels losses more strongly than gains)
mental accounting (separates cash as "safety" even if it’s inefficient)
hidden_concerns: worry about being underinsured since protection policies aren’t top-of-mind
concern about juggling finances with a very young child and future school costs without a clear system
